title: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome
authors: Rebecca M. Baron, Bruce D. Levy

items.title: Major Etiologies and Risk Factors for ARDS Development
items.content: ARDS is primarily caused by a small number of clinical disorders, with pneumonia and sepsis being the most common. Other significant causes include aspiration of gastric contents, trauma, multiple transfusions, and drug overdose. The risk of developing ARDS increases in patients with multiple predisposing conditions.
keyPoints: Most cases (>80%) are caused by a small number of disorders: pneumonia and sepsis (~40–60%), aspiration, trauma, multiple transfusions, and drug overdose.
Trauma-related ARDS often involves pulmonary contusion, multiple bone fractures, chest wall trauma/flail chest.
Risk factors include older age, chronic alcohol abuse, pancreatitis, severity of critical illness, and high APACHE II score ($ \geq $16) in trauma patients.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Incidence and Clinical Impact of ARDS
items.content: ARDS has a significant incidence rate, with an estimated 60 cases per 100,000 population annually before the COVID-19 pandemic. It accounts for approximately 10% of ICU admissions. The condition is associated with increased mortality risk and longer durations of mechanical ventilation in survivors.
keyPoints: Annual incidence was estimated at 60 cases per 100,000 population prior to COVID-19.
Approximately 10% of ICU admissions involve ARDS patients.
ARDS stages (mild, moderate, severe) are linked with mortality risk and duration of mechanical ventilation.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding the Exudative Phase of ARDS: Key Features and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: The exudative phase is characterized by alveolar capillary endothelial injury, leading to protein-rich edema in interstitial and alveolar spaces. This phase involves increased proinflammatory cytokines and lipid mediators, causing leukocyte recruitment and hyaline membrane formation. Pulmonary vascular injury with microthrombi also occurs early.
keyPoints: Occurs within the first 7 days after ARDS risk factor exposure
Symptoms can present from 12–36 hours to 5–7 days post-insult
Chest radiograph reveals opacities consistent with pulmonary edema, often involving at least three-quarters of lung fields
Differentiation from cardiogenic pulmonary edema is crucial; absence of cardiomegaly and pleural effusions in ARDS
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Proliferative Phase: Recovery Dynamics and Histological Changes in ARDS
items.content: The proliferative phase, spanning days 7 to 21, marks the initiation of lung repair. Type II pneumocytes proliferate, synthesizing new surfactant and differentiating into type I cells. Despite improvements, some patients may develop early pulmonary fibrosis.
keyPoints: Lasts from approximately day 7 to day 21
Characterized by a shift from neutrophil- to lymphocyte-predominant infiltrates
Histological signs of resolution include organization of alveolar exudates and lung repair initiation
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Fibrotic Phase: Long-term Implications and Increased Mortality Risk in ARDS
items.content: The fibrotic phase involves conversion of edema and inflammatory exudates to alveolar-duct and interstitial fibrosis. This leads to disrupted acinar architecture, emphysema-like changes, and increased risk of pneumothorax. Pulmonary hypertension and reduced lung compliance are significant concerns.
keyPoints: Occurs in some patients 3–4 weeks post-initial injury
Histological features include extensive fibrosis and vascular occlusion
Associated with increased mortality risk
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Importance of Low Tidal Volume Ventilation in ARDS Management
items.content: Low tidal volume ventilation significantly reduces mortality rates in ARDS patients by minimizing ventilator-induced lung injury.
keyPoints: Ventilator-induced lung injury can result from volutrauma and atelectrauma.
A large-scale trial showed a reduction in mortality rate with low $ V_T $ (6 mL/kg predicted body weight) compared to conventional $ V_T $ (12 mL/kg predicted body weight).
Mortality rates were 31% for low $ V_T $ patients versus 40% for conventional $ V_T $ patients.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Optimizing PEEP to Prevent Atelectrauma in ARDS
items.content: Adjusting positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) is crucial for minimizing alveolar collapse and improving oxygenation without causing overdistention.
keyPoints: PEEP helps prevent atelectrauma by maintaining alveolar patency.
No consensus exists on the optimal PEEP setting; various methods include using ARDS Network trial tables, pressure-volume curves, or esophageal pressures.
A recent trial showed no benefit of routine use of esophageal pressure-guided PEEP over empirical high PEEP- $ F_{IO_2} $ titration.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.title: Prone Positioning for Severe ARDS Patients
items.content: Prone positioning can significantly reduce mortality in patients with severe ARDS by improving oxygenation.
keyPoints: A 2013 trial demonstrated a reduction in 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16.0% with prone positioning for PaO_{2}/FiO_{2} <150 mmHg.
Prone positioning requires experienced critical care teams due to potential hazards like accidental extubation and orthopedic injury.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.title: Recruitment Maneuvers and ECMO in ARDS Management
items.content: Recruitment maneuvers, which transiently increase PEEP to recruit atelectatic lung, may improve oxygenation but have not shown a mortality benefit. In fact, they can increase mortality when combined with higher baseline PEEP settings. ECMO as an initial therapy for severe ARDS is not superior to its use as a rescue strategy after standard management failure.
keyPoints: Recruitment maneuvers do not establish a mortality benefit and may increase mortality with high baseline PEEP.
ECMO improved mortality in the UK study but only when used as a rescue therapy, not initially.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.title: Fluid Management and Neuromuscular Blockade in ARDS
items.content: Aggressive fluid management to reduce left atrial filling pressures with diuretics can minimize pulmonary edema, improve lung mechanics, and shorten ICU stay. In severe ARDS, early neuromuscular blockade for 48 hours has shown increased survival and ventilator-free days without increasing ICU-acquired paresis.
keyPoints: Fluid restriction and diuretics reduce left atrial filling pressures, minimizing pulmonary edema and improving outcomes.
Early neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium besylate for 48 hours increases survival in severe ARDS without increasing ICU-acquired paresis.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.title: Use of Low-Dose Hydrocortisone in ARDS-Associated Conditions
items.content: While routine use of glucocorticoids is not recommended for ARDS, low-dose hydrocortisone (200 mg over 24 hours) may be considered in specific conditions often associated with ARDS, such as refractory hypotension due to sepsis and severe community-acquired pneumonia.
keyPoints: Low-dose hydrocortisone: 200 mg over 24 h
Indications include refractory hypotension from septic shock and severe community-acquired pneumonia
Supported by recent guidelines (e.g., 2021 Surviving Sepsis guidelines) and studies showing mortality benefit
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.title: Considerations for Other Therapies in ARDS Management
items.content: Surfactant replacement, pulmonary vasodilators like inhaled nitric oxide and epoprostenol sodium, and early neuromuscular blockade have not consistently shown benefits in improving survival or reducing time on mechanical ventilation in ARDS patients.
keyPoints: Surfactant replacement and other medical therapies: disappointing results
Pulmonary vasodilators (inhaled nitric oxide, epoprostenol sodium): transient oxygenation improvement but no survival benefit
Early neuromuscular blockade: mortality benefit in one trial not replicated, suggesting caution in routine use
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.title: Understanding Mortality Trends in ARDS
items.content: Hospital mortality rates for ARDS vary by severity, with higher rates associated with more severe cases. Nonpulmonary causes, such as sepsis and organ failure, are the primary contributors to mortality.
keyPoints: Mortality estimates from LUNG SAFE trial: 34.9% (mild), 40.3% (moderate), 46.1% (severe) ARDS
>80% of deaths in ARDS due to nonpulmonary causes like sepsis and organ failure
Trend towards improved outcomes over time, likely due to better management of septic patients and multiple organ failures
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.title: Risk Factors and Predictors for ARDS Mortality
items.content: Advanced age and preexisting conditions significantly increase mortality risk in ARDS. Severity, as defined by the Berlin definition, is a key predictor of mortality.
keyPoints: Mortality risk ~60% for patients aged >75 years; ~20% for those <45 years
Patients >60 with sepsis have threefold higher mortality than those <60
Preexisting conditions like chronic liver disease, alcohol abuse, and immunosuppression increase risk
Direct lung injury causes nearly double the mortality compared to indirect causes
Severity of ARDS (Berlin definition) predicts increased mortality; little additional value from other parameters like PEEP ≥10 cmH2O or compliance ≤40 mL/cmH2O
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which two conditions are the most common causes of ARDS, accounting for approximately 40-60% of cases?
options: Aspiration of gastric contents and trauma
items.explanation: Pneumonia and sepsis are the most common causes, accounting for 40-60% of ARDS cases.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which surgical condition is NOT among the most frequently reported in trauma patients with ARDS?
items.explanation: Head trauma is a more rare cause of ARDS in trauma patients.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.question: What factor increases the risk of developing ARDS in trauma patients?
items.explanation: An APACHE II score ≥ 16 increases the risk of developing ARDS by 2.5-fold in trauma patients.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which cells are primarily injured during the exudative phase of ARDS?
options: Type II pneumocytes and alveolar macrophages
Alveolar capillary endothelial cells and type I pneumocytes
Bronchial epithelial cells and alveolar macrophages
items.explanation: In the exudative phase, injury occurs to alveolar capillary endothelial cells and type I pneumocytes.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in ARDS that can be indistinguishable from cardiogenic pulmonary edema?
items.explanation: Chest radiographs in ARDS often reveal opacities consistent with pulmonary edema, similar to cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: During which phase of ARDS does the proliferative process involving type II pneumocytes occur?
items.explanation: The proliferative phase involves the proliferation of type II pneumocytes along alveolar basement membranes.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which pathophysiological change is associated with increased pulmonary dead space in ARDS?
items.explanation: Microvascular occlusion leads to reductions in pulmonary arterial blood flow, increasing dead space.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: What is a primary reason for the recent reduction in ARDS mortality rates?
options: Increased use of high-flow nasal oxygen and non-invasive ventilation.
General advances in the care of critically ill patients.
Introduction of new pharmacological treatments specifically targeting ARDS.
Development of novel surgical techniques to manage underlying conditions.
items.explanation: Recent reductions in ARDS mortality rates are largely due to general advances in the care of critically ill patients.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: Which mechanism is NOT associated with ventilator-induced lung injury?
items.explanation: Ventilator-induced lung injury can arise from volutrauma, atelectrauma, and barotrauma; hypoxemia is not a mechanism of ventilator-induced injury.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: What was the mortality rate for patients in the low tidal volume group according to the ARDS Network trial?
items.explanation: The mortality rate was significantly lower in the low tidal volume patients at 31%.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.question: Which of the following is a benefit of prone positioning in severe ARDS?
options: Improved arterial oxygenation without mortality benefit.
Significant reduction in 28-day mortality for patients with PaO2/FiO2 <150 mmHg.
Reduction in the need for mechanical ventilation.
items.explanation: A significant reduction in 28-day mortality was observed with prone positioning for patients with severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150 mmHg).
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.question: Which of the following statements about recruitment maneuvers in ARDS is correct?
options: Recruitment maneuvers have been shown to reduce mortality when combined with higher baseline PEEP settings.
Recruitment maneuvers can increase oxygenation but may increase mortality if combined with high baseline PEEP.
Recruitment maneuvers are proven beneficial over standard modes of ventilation in ARDS management.
Recruitment maneuvers should be used routinely as they have a clear mortality benefit.
items.explanation: Recruitment maneuvers can increase oxygenation but may increase mortality when combined with higher baseline PEEP settings.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.question: What is the role of ECMO in ARDS management according to the passage?
options: ECMO has been proven superior as an initial treatment for all patients with severe ARDS.
ECMO showed improved mortality only when used initially, not as a rescue therapy.
ECMO may be beneficial as a rescue therapy for select adult patients with severe ARDS.
ECMO is ineffective and should not be considered in the management of ARDS.
items.explanation: ECMO has utility as a rescue therapy for select adult patients with severe ARDS, but initial use was not superior to its use after standard ARDS management failure.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.question: Which of the following statements about fluid management in ARDS is correct?
options: Fluid restriction and diuretics should be avoided to prevent hypotension.
Maintaining a low left atrial filling pressure can minimize pulmonary edema and improve outcomes.
Aggressive fluid resuscitation is recommended to maintain adequate organ perfusion.
Increased extravascular lung water has no impact on arterial oxygenation.
items.explanation: Maintaining a low left atrial filling pressure minimizes pulmonary edema and improves outcomes in ARDS management.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.question: What is the current recommendation regarding the use of glucocorticoids for treating ARDS?
options: Glucocorticoids are recommended as a routine treatment.
Current evidence supports the use of high-dose glucocorticoids in all ARDS patients.
Low-dose hydrocortisone is recommended for sepsis with refractory hypotension and severe community-acquired pneumonia, conditions often coexisting with ARDS.
Glucocorticoids are universally beneficial for reducing mortality in ARDS.
items.explanation: Current evidence does not support routine use of glucocorticoids in ARDS; however, low-dose hydrocortisone is recommended in specific conditions often coexisting with ARDS.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: Which of the following therapies has shown a mortality benefit in severe ARDS according to recent studies?
options: High PEEP combined with recruitment maneuvers.
items.explanation: Prone positioning was shown to improve mortality in severe ARDS in one randomized controlled clinical trial.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: What is the consensus on the use of pulmonary vasodilators such as inhaled nitric oxide and inhaled epoprostenol sodium for ARDS?
options: They improve survival and decrease time on mechanical ventilation.
Their use is universally recommended based on strong clinical evidence.
They can transiently improve oxygenation but have not shown to improve survival or reduce mechanical ventilation duration.
They are contraindicated in all ARDS patients.
items.explanation: Pulmonary vasodilators like inhaled nitric oxide and epoprostenol sodium can transiently improve oxygenation but have not been shown to improve survival or decrease time on mechanical ventilation.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.question: What was the hospital mortality estimate for severe ARDS according to the LUNG SAFE trial?
items.explanation: The LUNG SAFE trial reported a hospital mortality of 46.1% for severe ARDS.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for the majority of deaths in patients with ARDS?
items.explanation: More than 80% of deaths in ARDS are attributable to nonpulmonary causes, particularly sepsis and nonpulmonary organ failure.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which age group has a substantially higher mortality risk for ARDS?
items.explanation: Patients aged >75 years have a significantly higher mortality risk (~60%) compared to those <45 (~20%).
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.statement: The majority of ARDS cases are caused by a wide variety of medical and surgical conditions.
items.explanation: Most ARDS cases (>80%) are caused by a small number of clinical disorders, primarily pneumonia and sepsis.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: ARDS can be caused by both direct and indirect lung injuries.
items.explanation: Lung injury in ARDS may be direct (e.g., toxic inhalation) or indirect (e.g., sepsis).
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: The exudative phase of ARDS is characterized by the proliferation of type II pneumocytes.
items.explanation: Type II pneumocyte proliferation occurs during the proliferative phase, not the exudative phase.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Patients in the fibrotic phase of ARDS may experience increased risk of pneumothorax.
items.explanation: The fibrotic phase is associated with marked disruption of acinar architecture, leading to an increased risk of pneumothorax.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.statement: High-flow nasal cannula can provide low levels of PEEP.
items.explanation: High-flow nasal cannula may indeed provide low levels of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP).
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.statement: There is a consensus on the optimal method to set PEEP in ARDS.
items.explanation: Currently, there is no consensus on the optimal method to set PEEP due to inconclusive trial results.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.statement: Early neuromuscular blockade with cisatracurium besylate for 48 hours increases survival in patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS.
items.explanation: A subsequent trial showed no mortality benefit for early neuromuscular blockade in patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.subtopic: OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE
items.statement: Fluid restriction and diuretics are important for reducing left atrial filling pressures in ARDS management.
items.explanation: Aggressive attempts to reduce left atrial filling pressures with fluid restriction and diuretics should be an important aspect of ARDS management.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · OTHER STRATEGIES IN MECHANICAL VENTILATION | FLUID MANAGEMENT | NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE

items.statement: ECMO is recommended for all patients with ARDS.
items.explanation: ECMO may be beneficial in select patients with severe ARDS, not universally for all.

items.statement: There is a consensus on the optimal PEEP setting in ARDS.
items.explanation: As described, there is no consensus on the optimal PEEP setting in ARDS.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GLUCOCORTICOIDS | OTHER THERAPIES | RECOMMENDATIONS

items.statement: The severity of ARDS, as defined by the Berlin definition, does not predict increased mortality.
items.explanation: Increasing severity of ARDS predicts increased mortality according to the consensus Berlin definition.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Patients with ARDS from direct lung injury have a lower mortality rate compared to those with indirect causes.
items.explanation: Patients with ARDS arising from direct lung injury are nearly twice as likely to die as those with indirect causes.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · PROGNOSIS

items.assertion: ARDS can present with symptoms within 12–36 hours after the initial insult.
items.reason: The exudative phase usually encompasses the first 7 days of illness, and symptoms often appear early in this period.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the timing of symptom onset.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: Chest radiographs in ARDS always show cardiomegaly.
items.reason: Cardiomegaly is a feature that distinguishes cardiogenic pulmonary edema from ARDS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as chest x-rays in ARDS may not demonstrate cardiomegaly.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · CLINICAL COURSE AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: Prone positioning can lead to accidental endotracheal extubation.
items.reason: Repositioning critically ill patients in the prone position requires an experienced critical care team due to potential hazards.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why prone positioning can lead to complications like accidental extubation.
items.reference: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome · GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES | MANAGEMENT OF MECHANICAL VENTILATION
items.assertion: Low tidal volume ventilation is associated with higher mortality in ARDS patients.
items.reason: A large-scale trial showed a lower mortality rate with low tidal volume ventilation compared to conventional tidal volume.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the trial demonstrated that low tidal volume ventilation was associated with lower mortality.
